title: `podman run` fails when HOME set to invalid path and unprivileged system user specified as systemd `User=`
body:
### Issue Description

`podman run` fails to run a rootless service when `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is unset and an unprivileged, system user that has `HOME` set to an invalid path is used as `User=`.

At a guess, this appears to be caused due to the following behavior, described in [podman docs](https://docs.podman.io/en/stable/markdown/podman.1.html):

> In Rootless mode configuration files are read from XDG_CONFIG_HOME when specified, otherwise in the home directory of the user under $HOME/.config/containers

`podman run` fails to account for a missing `$HOME` dir. Lack of additional config files should not cause podman to fail. Default settings should... default.

Adding `Environment=XDG_CONFIG_HOME=/run/user/$TEST_UID` or `Environment=HOME=/run/user/$TEST_UID` to `[Service]` works around the bug. **Note**: these workarounds are effective even if the provided folders are empty, thus it is not the case that "Rootless mode configuration files" must be provided.

### Why is HOME set to an invalid path?

Because that's what [Debian policy requires](https://www.debian.org/doc/debian-policy/ch-opersys.html#non-existent-home-directories):

> The canonical non-existent home directory is `/nonexistent`. Users who should not have a home directory should have their home directory set to this value.

System users should not have a home directory for security reasons.

### Steps to reproduce the issue


```bash
# Create the unprivileged system user
useradd \
  --system \
  --add-subids-for-system \
  --shell /usr/sbin/nologin \
  --user-group \
  --no-create-home \
  --home-dir /nonexistent \
  --key UMASK=077 \
  test-user

# Capture the UID 
TEST_UID=$(id -u test-user)

# Write the service file
cat << EOF > /etc/systemd/system/test-service.service
[Unit]
Description=Podman test service

[Service]
User=test-user
Environment=XDG_DATA_HOME=/run/user/$TEST_UID
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
Restart=on-failure
KillMode=mixed
Delegate=yes
Type=notify
NotifyAccess=all
SyslogIdentifier=%N

ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman rm -v -f -i --cidfile=/run/user/$TEST_UID/%N.cid
ExecStopPost=-/usr/bin/podman rm -v -f -i --cidfile=/run/user/$TEST_UID/%N.cid
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman run \
  --name myservice \
  --cidfile=/run/user/$TEST_UID/%N.cid \
  --replace \
  --rm \
  --cgroups=split \
  --sdnotify=conmon \
  --detach \
  docker.io/bash:5.3.0 \
  -c 'exec tail -f /dev/null'

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Reload systemd and start the service (it will fail)
systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl start test-service.service

# See errors in log
journalctl --boot --pager-end --unit test-service
```



### Describe the results you received

```
Jul 21 07:20:20 prime systemd[1]: test-service.service: Scheduled restart job, restart counter is at 3.
Jul 21 07:20:20 prime systemd[1]: Starting test-service.service...
Jul 21 07:20:20 prime test-service[686581]: cannot resolve /nonexistent: lstat /nonexistent: no such file or directory
Jul 21 07:20:20 prime systemd[1]: test-service.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=1/FAILURE
Jul 21 07:20:20 prime test-service[686590]: cannot resolve /nonexistent: lstat /nonexistent: no such file or directory
Jul 21 07:20:20 prime systemd[1]: test-service.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.
Jul 21 07:20:20 prime systemd[1]: Failed to start test-service.service.
```

### Describe the results you expected

`podman run` succeeds without errors

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

```
$ uname -a
Linux prime 6.12.35+deb13-amd64 containers/image#1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.12.35-1 (2025-07-03) x86_64 GNU/Linux

$ systemctl --version
```

### Additional information

(none)

comments.body:
> [Service]
User=test-user

We generally don't support that because it doesn't actually create a proper user session which causes a lot of other problems, no proper cgroup management for example. There is more discussion in https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/20573 but it is not something.


Without proper XDG_RUNTIME_DIR set we default to /tmp which is not optimal and without HOME I am not sure how you would even expect podman to write any of its images since we default to writing in $HOME/.local/share/...?

There are some cases where we do indeed should not read the config files and don't error on this, i.e. https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23818 but I am not sure the regular podman should be expected to work without a proper HOME defined.
But yes see my comments there in general I agree that reading config files should never fail with ENOENT and instead we should ignore it instead.

> Without proper XDG_RUNTIME_DIR set

I can manually create sessions and set XDG_RUNTIME_DIR just fine. This is not what this bug report is about.

This issue report is about Podman demanding HOME be set to a valid path because it's read as a fallback for XDG_CONFIG_HOME, which doesn't make sense because an empty folder works just fine. 

Either fail on valid-but-empty HOME or stop failing on invalid HOME. I don't see a case for the current behavior.

> There is more discussion in https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/20573 but it is not something.

I have read through that entire discussion and all linked bugs and PRs before filing this issue. In fact, I'm in the process of trying to find a good set of workarounds for using a system user for running rootless containers.

I can already make it work; I'm trying to file bugs for the workarounds that should not be necessary (like this bug).

> without HOME I am not sure how you would even expect Podman to write any of its images since we default to writing in $HOME/.local/share/...?

[Podman documentation says](https://docs.podman.io/en/stable/markdown/podman.1.html#xdg-data-home):

> In Rootless mode images are pulled under XDG_DATA_HOME when specified, otherwise in the home directory of the user under $HOME/.local/share/containers/storage.

I can set XDG_DATA_HOME to whatever directory works for me. HOME is not necessary (or desired, because of security).

> but I am not sure the regular Podman should be expected to work without a proper HOME defined.

Why?

> Either fail on valid-but-empty HOME or stop failing on invalid HOME. I don't see a case for the current behavior.

Which is what I said in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23818 so I close this as dup then


> HOME is not necessary (or desired, because of security).

How is not having a valid $HOME any more secure than having it? If you still set all the XDG_ dirs then you still have many user writeable directories so I fail to see how this can be a security argument at all. The debina link you gave doesn't mention security either.

@Luap99 

> How is not having a valid $HOME any more secure than having it?

One fewer directory to secure?

>  If you still set all the XDG_ dirs then you still have many user writeable directories so I fail to see how this can be a security argument at all. The debina link you gave doesn't mention security either.

The main argument isn't so much "security", it's "all the tools for creating _system_ users do not create a home directory for them" and Podman (IMO, needlessly) adds another stumbling block to using system users.

[`adduser --system` docs](https://manpages.debian.org/bookworm/adduser/adduser.8.en.html):

> If no home directory is specified, the default home directory for a new system user is `/nonexistent`. This directory should never exist on any Debian system, and `adduser` will never create it automatically.

[`useradd --system` docs](https://manpages.debian.org/unstable/passwd/useradd.8.en.html)
> Note that `useradd` will not create a home directory for such a user, regardless of the default setting in `/etc/login.defs` (CREATE_HOME). You have to specify the `-m` options if you want a home directory for a system account to be created.

Ansible's `ansible.builtin.user` module also[ uses `useradd` under the hood](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/collections/ansible/builtin/user_module.html#notes), so setting [`system: true`](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/collections/ansible/builtin/user_module.html#parameter-system) will not create the home directory.

All the tools that I (and many, many others) regularly use to create system users will _intentionally_ not create a valid HOME directory for them. 

I don't understand why I'm being asked to defend the idea that "system users don't have valid HOME directories", it predates us both likely by decades. :)

It is not about defending I just don't buy the argument that this matters for security in any way as long as you still have to expose writeable directories for the user.

In any case like I said I agree this specific instance is a valid but it is already tracked in the other issue so there is no point in having dups here.
If there other $HOME access issues feel free to report them but I am not sure if all of them can be fixed. For example there are still many config files that only lookup in $HOME so you won't able to configure them then if you don't have $HOME/ https://github.com/containers/container-libs/issues/183 Sure again stuff that should be fixed eventually but it won't work for the moment.
Also looking at more code paths I see we at least create $HOME/.ssh/known_hosts for example when doing ssh connections. I am not aware of having a XDG_ directory preference for that one?

Podman needing a full user session for most of it things is in general already in full contrast to what a normal system user is  so by the point where you again expose a full systemd user session for this "system" user it seems to be basically a normal user with $HOME? And that does not seem particular more useful or secure  to me. But anyway no much point in arguing about such things.
